result.markdown:
---
title: "ARCH Models: Blair Approval Rating"
author: "Robert W. Walker"
date: "07/24/2026"
categories: [panel data, code, analysis]
image: "image.png"
---


# Introduction

## Overview

This document works through an ARCH modelling example using Tony Blair's
monthly approval ratings from June 1997 onward. Each section presents
**R** and **Stata** tabs side by side. The R tab is executed and results
are shown on the page; the Stata tab provides equivalent syntax for
reference.  It extends the ECM analysis to ARCH.

Variables in `tonyapp4.dta`:

| Variable   | Description                                    |
|------------|------------------------------------------------|
| `app`      | Blair approval rating (%)                      |
| `labecman` | % thinking Labour best manager of the economy  |
| `v911d`    | 9/11 dummy (differenced)                       |
| `petrold`  | Petrol crisis dummy (differenced)              |
| `casl`     | Iraq casualties (logged)                       |
| `casld`    | Iraq casualties logged, differenced            |
| `appd`     | `app`, first-differenced (pre-stored)          |
| `labecmand`| `labecman`, first-differenced (pre-stored)     |

---

## 1. Setup

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(haven)     # read .dta files
library(zoo)       # yearmon monthly time index
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```

Attaching package: 'zoo'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    as.Date, as.Date.numeric
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tseries)   # adf.test  (Augmented Dickey-Fuller)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod':
  method            from
  as.zoo.data.frame zoo 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
library(fracdiff)  # fracdiff  (ARFIMA models)
library(rugarch)   # ugarchspec / ugarchfit  (ARCH/GARCH)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Loading required package: parallel
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)   # plotting
```
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
* All procedures used here are built into Stata.
* No package installation is required.
```

:::

---

## 2. Load Data

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df <- read_dta("./data/tonyapp4.dta")
```
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
use "tonyapp4.dta", clear
```

:::

---

## 3. Create Monthly Time Index

Stata's `%tm` format counts months from January 1960 (month 0), so June
1997 = month 450. In R we use `zoo::yearmon` to represent the same
monthly series.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n          <- nrow(df)
df$counter <- 0:(n - 1)

# as.yearmon("1997-06") is June 1997; each step is 1/12 of a year
df$month   <- as.yearmon("1997-06") + df$counter / 12
```
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
* Create a running counter 0, 1, 2, â€¦ then shift to Stata %tm month number
egen counter = fill(0 1 2 3)
gen  month   = counter + 450
format month %tm
tsset month
```

:::

---

## 4. Blair Approval Rating

### 4.1 Exploratory Plot

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(df, aes(x = month, y = app)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(title = "Blair Approval Rating",
       x = "Month", y = "Approval (%)")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/plot-app-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
label variable app "Blair Approval Rating"
twoway line app month
```

:::

### 4.2 Stationarity Tests (Augmented Dickey-Fuller)

The plot suggests the series is non-stationary. We confirm with ADF tests
on the level and the first difference.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
adf.test(df$app)        # level
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  df$app
Dickey-Fuller = -2.6626, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.302
alternative hypothesis: stationary
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
adf.test(diff(df$app))  # first difference
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in adf.test(diff(df$app)): p-value smaller than printed p-value
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  diff(df$app)
Dickey-Fuller = -5.6329, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary
```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
dfuller app
dfuller d.app
```

:::

---

## 5. ARFIMA Models

With `d.app` apparently stationary, we test for fractional integration.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
dapp <- diff(df$app)

# ARFIMA(0, d, 0)
fracdiff(dapp)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

Call:
  fracdiff(x = dapp) 

Coefficients:
           d 
4.583013e-05 
sigma[eps] = 4.596884 
a list with components:
 [1] "log.likelihood"  "n"               "msg"             "d"              
 [5] "ar"              "ma"              "covariance.dpq"  "fnormMin"       
 [9] "sigma"           "stderror.dpq"    "correlation.dpq" "h"              
[13] "d.tol"           "M"               "hessian.dpq"     "length.w"       
[17] "residuals"       "fitted"          "call"           
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ARFIMA(1, d, 0)
fracdiff(dapp, nar = 1)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: unable to compute correlation matrix; maybe change 'h'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

Call:
  fracdiff(x = dapp, nar = 1) 

*** Warning during (fdcov) fit: unable to compute correlation matrix; maybe change 'h'

Coefficients:
            d            ar 
 4.583013e-05 -1.276140e-01 
sigma[eps] = 4.581019 
a list with components:
 [1] "log.likelihood"  "n"               "msg"             "d"              
 [5] "ar"              "ma"              "covariance.dpq"  "fnormMin"       
 [9] "sigma"           "stderror.dpq"    "correlation.dpq" "h"              
[13] "d.tol"           "M"               "hessian.dpq"     "length.w"       
[17] "residuals"       "fitted"          "call"           
```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
arfima d.app              /* ARFIMA(0, d, 0)  */
arfima d.app, ar(1)       /* ARFIMA(1, d, 0)  */
```

:::

The Stata command `arfima d.app d.v911 d.petrol, ma(1)` allows external
regressors directly. `fracdiff` does not support `xreg`, so the R
equivalent pre-filters `d.app` by regressing on the covariates and then
fits ARFIMA on the residuals:

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Compute differenced regressors (adjust column names to match your .dta)
dv911   <- diff(df$v911)
dpetrol <- diff(df$petrol)

# Pre-filter: regress d.app on covariates, then fit ARFIMA(0,d,1) on residuals
pre_fit  <- lm(dapp ~ dv911 + dpetrol)
fracdiff(residuals(pre_fit), nma = 1)  # ARFIMA(0, d, 1) on residuals
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: unable to compute correlation matrix; maybe change 'h'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

Call:
  fracdiff(x = residuals(pre_fit), nma = 1) 

*** Warning during (fdcov) fit: unable to compute correlation matrix; maybe change 'h'

Coefficients:
           d           ma 
4.583013e-05 6.353972e-02 
sigma[eps] = 4.200936 
a list with components:
 [1] "log.likelihood"  "n"               "msg"             "d"              
 [5] "ar"              "ma"              "covariance.dpq"  "fnormMin"       
 [9] "sigma"           "stderror.dpq"    "correlation.dpq" "h"              
[13] "d.tol"           "M"               "hessian.dpq"     "length.w"       
[17] "residuals"       "fitted"          "call"           
```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
arfima d.app d.v911 d.petrol, ma(1)   /* ARFIMA(0,d,1) with regressors */
```

:::

---

## 6. Labour Economic Management

### 6.1 Stationarity Tests

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
adf.test(df$labecman)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  df$labecman
Dickey-Fuller = -1.8498, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.639
alternative hypothesis: stationary
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
adf.test(diff(df$labecman))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in adf.test(diff(df$labecman)): p-value smaller than printed p-value
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  diff(df$labecman)
Dickey-Fuller = -4.7619, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary
```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
label variable labecman "Labour Best Eco Mng"
dfuller labecman
dfuller d.labecman
```

:::

### 6.2 Overlay Plot

Both series appear non-stationary and are plotted together to inspect
potential cointegration.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(df, aes(x = month)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = app,      colour = "Blair Approval")) +
  geom_line(aes(y = labecman, colour = "Labour Best Eco Mng")) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Blair Approval"       = "#2c7bb6",
               "Labour Best Eco Mng" = "#d7191c")
  ) +
  labs(title  = "Approval & Labour Economic Management",
       x = "Month", y = NULL, colour = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/plot-coint-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
twoway line app month || line labecman month
```

:::

### 6.3 Cointegration Residuals (Engle-Granger)

Regress `app` on `labecman`; if the residuals are I(0), the two series
are cointegrated.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
reg_coint       <- lm(app ~ labecman, data = df)
df$ecm_labecman <- residuals(reg_coint)
adf.test(df$ecm_labecman)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  df$ecm_labecman
Dickey-Fuller = -2.5805, Lag order = 4, p-value = 0.336
alternative hypothesis: stationary
```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
reg app labecman
predict ecm_labecman, resid
dfuller ecm_labecman
```

:::

---

## 7. Error Correction Model (OLS)

The ECM takes `d.app` as the dependent variable. The levels `L.app` and
`L.labecman` provide the error-correction terms.

### 7.1 Construct Lag and Difference Variables

Stata computes lags and differences on the fly with `L.` and `D.`
operators. In R we create them explicitly.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df$dapp       <- c(NA, diff(df$app))
df$dlabecman  <- c(NA, diff(df$labecman))
df$L_app      <- c(NA, head(df$app,      -1))
df$L_labecman <- c(NA, head(df$labecman, -1))

# L2D.casl = second lag of first difference of casl
dcasl      <- c(NA, diff(df$casl))
df$L2Dcasl <- c(NA, NA, head(dcasl, -2))

# L.casl used later in the ARCH variance equation
df$L_casl  <- c(NA, head(df$casl, -1))
```
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
* Stata resolves d.app, L.app, L2D.casl, etc. automatically â€” no setup needed.
label var v911d   "911 differenced"
label var petrold "Petrol Crisis differenced"
label var casld   "Iraq casualties Logged differenced"
label var casl    "Iraq casualties Logged"
```

:::

### 7.2 OLS Regression

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
reg_ecm <- lm(dapp ~ dlabecman + L_app + L_labecman +
                v911d + petrold + L2Dcasl,
              data = df)
summary(reg_ecm)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

Call:
lm(formula = dapp ~ dlabecman + L_app + L_labecman + v911d + 
    petrold + L2Dcasl, data = df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-9.035 -2.336 -0.075  1.910 11.749 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
(Intercept)  4.89260    1.95127   2.507  0.01392 * 
dlabecman    0.32033    0.10680   2.999  0.00348 **
L_app       -0.14727    0.05900  -2.496  0.01434 * 
L_labecman   0.12226    0.07665   1.595  0.11411   
v911d        9.07926    2.72946   3.326  0.00127 **
petrold     -5.59628    2.90474  -1.927  0.05712 . 
L2Dcasl     -2.13708    1.19284  -1.792  0.07649 . 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 3.817 on 92 degrees of freedom
  (3 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.3315,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.2879 
F-statistic: 7.602 on 6 and 92 DF,  p-value: 1.219e-06
```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
reg d.app d.labecman L.app L.labecman v911d petrold L2D.casl
```

:::

### 7.3 ARCH Effects Test

Autocorrelation in the **squared** OLS residuals signals ARCH effects.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Align residuals back into df by row index
df$resid_ols <- NA
df$resid_ols[as.integer(rownames(model.frame(reg_ecm)))] <- residuals(reg_ecm)
df$resid_ols_sq <- df$resid_ols^2

resid_sq_clean <- na.omit(df$resid_ols_sq)
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf( resid_sq_clean, lag.max = 20, main = "ACF  â€” Squared OLS Residuals")
pacf(resid_sq_clean, lag.max = 20, main = "PACF â€” Squared OLS Residuals")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/ecm-resid-sq-1.png){width=768}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
predict resid_ols, res
gen resid_ols_sq = resid_ols * resid_ols
corrgram resid_ols_sq
```

:::

---

## 8. ARCH(1) Model

We now account for conditional heteroskedasticity. Iraq casualties (logged,
lagged) enter the variance equation as an external predictor of volatility.

::: {.callout-note}
**Stata vs R â€” variance specification**  
Stata's `het()` option enters variance regressors *multiplicatively* in
log-linear form:
$$\sigma^2_t = \exp(\gamma_0 + \gamma_1 \cdot \text{L.casl})\,(1 + \alpha_1 \varepsilon^2_{t-1})$$
`rugarch`'s `external.regressors` in the variance model enters them
*additively*:
$$\sigma^2_t = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon^2_{t-1} + \eta \cdot \text{L.casl}$$
Coefficients will differ in scale; the substantive interpretation is the same.
:::

### 8.1 Fit the Model

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Drop rows with any NA across required columns
arch_vars <- c("month", "dapp", "dlabecman", "L_app", "L_labecman",
               "v911d", "petrold", "L2Dcasl", "L_casl")
arch_df   <- na.omit(df[, arch_vars])

y_arch    <- arch_df$dapp
mean_xreg <- as.matrix(arch_df[, c("dlabecman", "L_app", "L_labecman",
                                    "v911d", "petrold", "L2Dcasl")])
var_xreg  <- as.matrix(arch_df[, "L_casl", drop = FALSE])

spec_arch <- ugarchspec(
  variance.model = list(
    model               = "sGARCH",
    garchOrder          = c(1, 0),   # ARCH(1): one alpha, no beta
    external.regressors = var_xreg   # het(L.casl)
  ),
  mean.model = list(
    armaOrder           = c(0, 0),
    include.mean        = TRUE,
    external.regressors = mean_xreg
  ),
  distribution.model = "norm"
)

fit_arch <- ugarchfit(spec = spec_arch, data = y_arch)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in .sgarchfit(spec = spec, data = data, out.sample = out.sample, : 
ugarchfit-->waring: using less than 100 data
 points for estimation
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
show(fit_arch)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

```


:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
* appd and labecmand are pre-differenced columns already in the dataset
arch appd labecmand L.app L.labecman v911d petrold L2D.casl, ///
     het(L.casl) arch(1)
```

:::

### 8.2 Residual Diagnostics

If the ARCH model is well-specified, neither the residuals nor their
squares should show significant autocorrelation.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
resid_arch    <- residuals(fit_arch)
resid_arch_sq <- resid_arch^2

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
acf( resid_arch,    lag.max = 20, main = "ACF  â€” ARCH Residuals")
pacf(resid_arch,    lag.max = 20, main = "PACF â€” ARCH Residuals")
acf( resid_arch_sq, lag.max = 20, main = "ACF  â€” Squared ARCH Residuals")
pacf(resid_arch_sq, lag.max = 20, main = "PACF â€” Squared ARCH Residuals")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/arch-resid-1.png){width=768}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
predict resid, res
corrgram resid

gen resid_sq = resid * resid
corrgram resid_sq
```

:::

### 8.3 Conditional Variance

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sigma2    <- sigma(fit_arch)^2   # sigma() returns std dev; square for variance
sigma2_df <- data.frame(
  month  = arch_df$month,
  sigma2 = as.numeric(sigma2)
)

ggplot(sigma2_df, aes(x = month, y = sigma2)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(
    title = expression("Conditional Variance " * sigma^2 * " â€” ARCH(1) Model"),
    x = "Month", y = expression(sigma^2)
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/arch-sigma2-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
predict sigma2, variance
graph twoway line sigma2 month
```

:::

---

## 9. Digression: Random Walks

Random walks are a canonical non-stationary process. Their variance grows
over time â€” but does an ARCH model pick this up?

### 9.1 Simulate and Plot

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(1234)
r1 <- rnorm(n)       # one N(0,1) draw per observation
Y1 <- cumsum(r1)     # cumsum() = Stata's running sum()

rw_df <- data.frame(month = df$month, Y1 = Y1)
ggplot(rw_df, aes(x = month, y = Y1)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2c7bb6") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  labs(title = "Simulated Random Walk", x = "Month", y = "Y1")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/rw-sim-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
set seed 1234
drawnorm r1
generate Y1 = sum(r1)    /* sum() in Stata is a running/cumulative sum */

twoway line Y1 month, yline(0)
```

:::

### 9.2 ARCH(1) on the Random Walk

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
rw_spec <- ugarchspec(
  variance.model     = list(model = "sGARCH", garchOrder = c(1, 0)),
  mean.model         = list(armaOrder = c(0, 0), include.mean = TRUE),
  distribution.model = "norm"
)
rw_fit <- ugarchfit(spec = rw_spec, data = Y1)
show(rw_fit)
```

:::

### 9.3 Conditional Variance of the Random Walk

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sigma1_rw   <- sigma(rw_fit)^2
sigma_rw_df <- data.frame(
  month     = df$month[seq_along(sigma1_rw)],
  sigma1_rw = as.numeric(sigma1_rw)
)

ggplot(sigma_rw_df, aes(x = month, y = sigma1_rw)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(
    title = expression("Random Walk: Conditional Variance " * sigma[RW]^2 * " â€” ARCH(1)"),
    x = "Month", y = expression(sigma[RW]^2)
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/rw-sigma-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


### Stata

```{.stata}
predict sigma1_rw, variance
graph twoway line sigma1_rw month
```

:::
